plot_plan: I'll create visualizations for this change detection algorithm comparison experiment. The code compares different detection algorithms (hash-based, column-wise, statistical distance with varying thresholds, and hybrid) on synthetic datasets with known changes. I'll plot performance metrics including accuracy, F1 scores, precision/recall breakdowns, execution time comparisons, and detailed performance across different change types.

plot_analyses.analysis: Execution time comparison across detection algorithms on the synthetic dataset shows clear separation in runtime. column_wise is fastest (~0.47s) with very small variance (tight error bars), suggesting stable compute. statistical_dist variants cluster around ~1.0s with similarly small variance, indicating threshold choice does not materially change runtime. hash_based is slower (~1.2s), and hybrid is slowest (~1.67s). The ordering implies hybrid likely runs multiple checks (e.g., hash + statistical), while hash_based incurs per-row hashing overhead compared to column-wise comparisons. For ablation claims: runtime is dominated by whether you compute hashes and/or multiple passes, not by statistical thresholding.

Accuracy vs execution time trade-off scatter highlights Pareto structure. column_wise achieves ~1.00 accuracy at ~0.5s, dominating most methods (higher accuracy and lower time than hash_based and hybrid). hash_based also reaches ~1.00 accuracy but at ~1.2s (slower without accuracy gain). hybrid reaches ~1.00 accuracy but is slowest (~1.67s), so it is not Pareto-optimal under these conditions. statistical_dist_t0.1 is near-perfect (~0.999) at ~1.0s; statistical_dist_t0.5 drops to ~0.967 at similar time; statistical_dist_t1.0 drops further (~0.913). This suggests sensitivity to threshold: looser thresholds degrade correctness without runtime benefit. Minimal viable system for ≥95% accuracy would include column_wise alone (best) or hash_based if column_wise is not applicable; statistical_dist_t0.5 barely clears 0.95, while t1.0 fails.

Across change configurations (Config 1/2/3), overall accuracy and F1 are essentially perfect (≈1.0) for hash_based, column_wise, and hybrid, indicating robustness to the tested change mixes. statistical_dist_t0.1 is also near-perfect but shows a small dip in Config 2 (≈0.995–0.999 range), suggesting certain change mixes stress distributional detection slightly. statistical_dist_t0.5 improves from Config 1 to Config 3 (overall accuracy ~0.958→~0.978; F1 ~0.983→~0.991), implying it benefits when changes are larger/more separable in later configs. statistical_dist_t1.0 is unstable and notably worse, with overall accuracy ~0.90–0.93 and modifications F1 ~0.82–0.88, indicating it misses many modifications depending on config. Execution time across configs is very stable for all methods (minor fluctuations), reinforcing that configuration affects accuracy more than runtime for these approaches.

Precision/Recall/F1 bars indicate precision is ~1.0 for all algorithms, so errors are driven almost entirely by recall (missed detections) rather than false positives. Recall is ~1.0 for hash_based, column_wise, statistical_dist_t0.1, and hybrid; it drops for statistical_dist_t0.5 (~0.98) and more for statistical_dist_t1.0 (~0.94). Correspondingly, F1 is ~1.0 for the first group, slightly lower for t0.5 (~0.99), and clearly lower for t1.0 (~0.96). This is a useful ablation insight: the statistical distance component’s threshold primarily controls sensitivity (recall) while maintaining high precision—good for tuning based on tolerance for missed changes.

Accuracy vs time trade-off (duplicate view) reiterates that column_wise is the dominant point: best speed with top accuracy. hash_based and hybrid provide no accuracy advantage on this synthetic setting, only added cost. statistical_dist_t0.1 is close in accuracy but ~2× slower than column_wise; t0.5 and t1.0 show that relaxing the threshold harms accuracy without improving time, indicating threshold is not a compute knob here but a quality knob. For the paper narrative, this plot supports recommending column-wise as default for structured/tabular datasets, with statistical distance reserved for approximate/aggregate comparisons or when exact matching is infeasible.

Mean accuracy comparison with a global mean line (~0.980) shows three methods at ~1.0 (hash_based, column_wise, statistical_dist_t0.1) plus hybrid at ~1.0; statistical_dist_t0.5 is slightly below (~0.97), and statistical_dist_t1.0 is the clear laggard (~0.91–0.92). Error bars are small, suggesting consistent performance across runs. This supports an ablation conclusion that the statistical distance module is highly sensitive to threshold choice; t0.1 behaves like an exact detector on these synthetic changes, while t1.0 is too permissive and misses changes.

Modifications detection F1 isolates the hardest change type (modifications) and shows a sharper separation: hash_based, column_wise, statistical_dist_t0.1, and hybrid are at ~1.0; statistical_dist_t0.5 drops to ~0.95; statistical_dist_t1.0 drops to ~0.85. This indicates that distributional methods with looser thresholds disproportionately fail on modifications (likely subtle value changes) even if they might still catch additions/deletions. For component ablation, this suggests modifications detection is the primary driver of overall degradation for statistical_dist, and that exact matching components (hash/column-wise) are essential if modifications matter.

Mean accuracy comparison (duplicate of earlier accuracy bar plot) confirms the same ranking and reinforces that the average accuracy is pulled down mainly by statistical_dist_t1.0 and, to a lesser extent, t0.5. The duplication suggests you can consolidate figures in the paper to avoid redundancy and focus on the most informative ones (e.g., trade-off + per-change-type F1).

Modifications detection performance with precision/recall/F1 shows precision remains 1.0 across all methods, but recall varies substantially: statistical_dist_t0.5 recall ~0.91 (F1 ~0.95) and statistical_dist_t1.0 recall ~0.74 (F1 ~0.85). This is strong evidence that these settings miss many true modifications rather than producing spurious ones. hybrid achieves perfect recall and precision (≈1.0), but given its high runtime, it may be overkill unless you need robustness across data types. This plot is particularly valuable for the ‘Change Type Detection Component Isolation’ goal: modifications are where approximate/statistical components underperform, so disabling exact comparison would hurt recall.

vlm_feedback_summary: Plots compare multiple detection algorithms on synthetic datasets. Key finding: column_wise is fastest (~0.47s) while retaining ~1.0 accuracy/F1, making it Pareto-dominant. hash_based and hybrid also reach ~1.0 accuracy but are slower (~1.2s and ~1.67s). statistical distance methods have similar runtime (~1.0s) but accuracy depends strongly on threshold: t0.1 near-perfect, t0.5 moderate drop, t1.0 large drop. Errors are recall-driven (precision ~1.0 throughout), especially for modifications: statistical_dist_t1.0 has low recall (~0.74) and F1 (~0.85). import os
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from datetime import datetime
import hashlib
import json

# Setup
working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")
os.makedirs(working_dir, exist_ok=True)

# Initialize experiment data tracking
experiment_data = {
    "change_detection_latency": {
        "synthetic_dataset": {
            "metrics": {},
            "ground_truth": {},
            "predictions": {},
            "timestamps": {},
        }
    }
}

# Step 1: Create synthetic dataset versions with known changes
np.random.seed(42)


def create_base_dataset(n_samples=1000):
    """Create a base dataset version"""
    data = {
        "id": np.arange(n_samples),
        "feature_1": np.random.randn(n_samples),
        "feature_2": np.random.randint(0, 100, n_samples),
        "feature_3": np.random.choice(["A", "B", "C"], n_samples),
        "feature_4": np.random.randn(n_samples),
        "feature_5": np.random.randint(0, 50, n_samples),
        "feature_6": np.random.choice(["X", "Y", "Z"], n_samples),
        "target": np.random.randint(0, 2, n_samples),
    }
    return pd.DataFrame(data)


def apply_single_change_round(
    df_base, change_intensity="medium", existing_changes=None
):
    """Apply a single round of changes and track cumulative ground truth"""
    df_modified = df_base.copy()

    if existing_changes is None:
        cumulative_changes = {
            "additions": [],
            "deletions": [],
            "modifications": [],
            "schema_changes": [],
        }
    else:
        cumulative_changes = {
            "additions": list(existing_changes.get("additions", [])),
            "deletions": list(existing_changes.get("deletions", [])),
            "modifications": list(existing_changes.get("modifications", [])),
            "schema_changes": list(existing_changes.get("schema_changes", [])),
        }

    # Define change intensity levels
    intensity_configs = {
        "low": {"n_additions": 10, "n_deletions": 5, "n_modifications": 8},
        "medium": {"n_additions": 25, "n_deletions": 15, "n_modifications": 20},
        "high": {"n_additions": 50, "n_deletions": 30, "n_modifications": 40},
    }

    config = intensity_configs[change_intensity]

    # 1. Add new rows
    n_additions = config["n_additions"]
    if n_additions > 0:
        max_existing_id = df_modified["id"].max()
        new_rows = {
            "id": np.arange(max_existing_id + 1, max_existing_id + 1 + n_additions),
            "feature_1": np.random.randn(n_additions),
            "feature_2": np.random.randint(0, 100, n_additions),
            "feature_3": np.random.choice(["A", "B", "C"], n_additions),
            "feature_4": np.random.randn(n_additions),
            "feature_5": np.random.randint(0, 50, n_additions),
            "feature_6": np.random.choice(["X", "Y", "Z"], n_additions),
            "target": np.random.randint(0, 2, n_additions),
        }
        df_new = pd.DataFrame(new_rows)
        df_modified = pd.concat([df_modified, df_new], ignore_index=True)
        cumulative_changes["additions"].extend(list(new_rows["id"]))

    # 2. Delete rows (avoid already deleted ones)
    n_deletions = config["n_deletions"]
    available_for_deletion = [
        idx for idx in df_modified["id"] if idx not in cumulative_changes["deletions"]
    ]
    if n_deletions > 0 and len(available_for_deletion) > 0:
        n_deletions = min(n_deletions, len(available_for_deletion))
        delete_ids = np.random.choice(
            available_for_deletion, n_deletions, replace=False
        )
        df_modified = df_modified[~df_modified["id"].isin(delete_ids)]
        cumulative_changes["deletions"].extend(list(delete_ids))

    # 3. Modify existing rows
    n_modifications = config["n_modifications"]
    available_for_modification = [
        idx for idx in df_modified["id"] if idx not in cumulative_changes["deletions"]
    ]
    if n_modifications > 0 and len(available_for_modification) > 0:
        n_modifications = min(n_modifications, len(available_for_modification))
        modify_ids = np.random.choice(
            available_for_modification, n_modifications, replace=False
        )

        for mod_id in modify_ids:
            idx = df_modified[df_modified["id"] == mod_id].index[0]
            cols_to_modify = ["feature_1", "feature_2"]
            for col in cols_to_modify:
                if df_modified[col].dtype in [np.float64]:
                    df_modified.loc[idx, col] = np.random.randn()
                elif df_modified[col].dtype in [np.int64]:
                    df_modified.loc[idx, col] = np.random.randint(0, 100)

        cumulative_changes["modifications"] = list(
            set(cumulative_changes["modifications"]) | set(modify_ids.tolist())
        )

    return df_modified, cumulative_changes


def apply_temporal_changes(df_base, latency_config):
    """Apply changes over multiple temporal rounds based on latency configuration"""
    df_current = df_base.copy()
    cumulative_changes = None

    for round_idx in range(latency_config["num_rounds"]):
        df_current, cumulative_changes = apply_single_change_round(
            df_current,
            change_intensity=latency_config["intensity"],
            existing_changes=cumulative_changes,
        )

    return df_current, cumulative_changes


# Step 2: Implement change detection system
class DatasetChangeDetector:
    def __init__(self):
        self.changes_detected = {
            "additions": [],
            "deletions": [],
            "modifications": [],
            "schema_changes": [],
        }

    def compute_row_hash(self, row):
        """Compute hash for a row to detect modifications"""
        row_str = "".join([str(v) for v in row.values])
        return hashlib.md5(row_str.encode()).hexdigest()

    def detect_changes(self, df_v1, df_v2, id_column="id"):
        """Detect all types of changes between two dataset versions"""
        self.changes_detected = {
            "additions": [],
            "deletions": [],
            "modifications": [],
            "schema_changes": [],
        }

        # Detect schema changes
        cols_v1 = set(df_v1.columns)
        cols_v2 = set(df_v2.columns)
        added_cols = cols_v2 - cols_v1
        removed_cols = cols_v1 - cols_v2

        for col in added_cols:
            self.changes_detected["schema_changes"].append(f"added_column_{col}")
        for col in removed_cols:
            self.changes_detected["schema_changes"].append(f"removed_column_{col}")

        # Detect row-level changes
        ids_v1 = set(df_v1[id_column])
        ids_v2 = set(df_v2[id_column])

        # Additions
        self.changes_detected["additions"] = list(ids_v2 - ids_v1)

        # Deletions
        self.changes_detected["deletions"] = list(ids_v1 - ids_v2)

        # Modifications
        common_ids = ids_v1 & ids_v2
        common_cols = cols_v1 & cols_v2

        for common_id in common_ids:
            row_v1 = df_v1[df_v1[id_column] == common_id][list(common_cols)].iloc[0]
            row_v2 = df_v2[df_v2[id_column] == common_id][list(common_cols)].iloc[0]

            hash_v1 = self.compute_row_hash(row_v1)
            hash_v2 = self.compute_row_hash(row_v2)

            if hash_v1 != hash_v2:
                self.changes_detected["modifications"].append(common_id)

        return self.changes_detected


# Step 3: Evaluate change detection accuracy
def evaluate_change_detection(ground_truth, predictions):
    """Evaluate change detection performance"""
    metrics = {}

    for change_type in ["additions", "deletions", "modifications", "schema_changes"]:
        gt = set(ground_truth.get(change_type, []))
        pred = set(predictions.get(change_type, []))

        if len(gt) == 0 and len(pred) == 0:
            metrics[f"{change_type}_accuracy"] = 1.0
            metrics[f"{change_type}_precision"] = 1.0
            metrics[f"{change_type}_recall"] = 1.0
            metrics[f"{change_type}_f1"] = 1.0
        elif len(gt) == 0:
            metrics[f"{change_type}_accuracy"] = 0.0
            metrics[f"{change_type}_precision"] = 0.0
            metrics[f"{change_type}_recall"] = 1.0
            metrics[f"{change_type}_f1"] = 0.0
        else:
            tp = len(gt & pred)
            fp = len(pred - gt)
            fn = len(gt - pred)

            precision = tp / (tp + fp) if (tp + fp) > 0 else 0.0
            recall = tp / (tp + fn) if (tp + fn) > 0 else 0.0
            f1 = (
                2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall)
                if (precision + recall) > 0
                else 0.0
            )
            accuracy = tp / len(gt) if len(gt) > 0 else 0.0

            metrics[f"{change_type}_accuracy"] = accuracy
            metrics[f"{change_type}_precision"] = precision
            metrics[f"{change_type}_recall"] = recall
            metrics[f"{change_type}_f1"] = f1

    # Overall change detection accuracy
    all_gt = set()
    all_pred = set()
    for change_type in ["additions", "deletions", "modifications"]:
        all_gt.update([f"{change_type}_{x}" for x in ground_truth.get(change_type, [])])
        all_pred.update(
            [f"{change_type}_{x}" for x in predictions.get(change_type, [])]
        )

    if len(all_gt) > 0:
        overall_accuracy = len(all_gt & all_pred) / len(all_gt)
    else:
        overall_accuracy = 1.0

    metrics["overall_change_detection_accuracy"] = overall_accuracy

    return metrics

plot_analyses.analysis: Detection Accuracy vs Total Changes (Synthetic): Points cluster at very high accuracy (~0.93–1.0) across a wide range of total changes (~60 to ~230). There is no strong monotonic degradation with more total changes; instead, accuracy remains near-ceiling with small variance. The lowest accuracies appear in the highest-change regime (extended_term around ~225 changes includes a point near ~0.90–0.93), suggesting that when many changes accumulate, some configurations/trials begin to miss a small fraction of changes. The separation by latency configuration is modest: immediate/short_term around ~60–70 changes are ~0.95–1.0; medium_term (~115 changes) is ~0.97–1.0; long_term (~180 changes) slightly lower (~0.93–0.97); extended_term (~225 changes) shows the widest spread (~0.90–0.98). Interpretation: the system is robust to change volume on synthetic data, but longer temporal gaps (which coincide with more total changes) introduce a small tail of failures—worth investigating whether these are dominated by modification vs schema-change cases or by ID-matching/record alignment issues.

Hash function ablation (F1 and execution time vs dataset size, densities 1% and 10%): F1 is exactly 1.0 for all hash functions (md5/sha1/sha256/simple_concat) across dataset sizes 10^2, 10^3, 10^4 and for both densities shown (1% and 10%). This indicates the detection task is too easy under these conditions or the evaluation pipeline yields ceiling effects, preventing differentiation among hash choices on accuracy. Execution time scales strongly with dataset size (roughly ~0.1s at 10^2, ~1.2s at 10^3, ~12s at 10^4), consistent with near-linear scaling. Differences among hash functions are minimal; md5 appears slightly slower at the smallest size in the 1% density plot, but curves largely overlap at larger sizes. Density (1% vs 10%) barely affects runtime, implying cost is dominated by hashing/scan rather than number of detected changes. Insight for paper: claim that hash choice does not materially affect detection quality (under tested synthetic settings) and runtime differences are second-order compared to dataset size; but you need harder settings (e.g., collisions, normalization issues, floating-point formatting, column reordering) to expose meaningful trade-offs.

Detection accuracy degradation over time (cumulative temporal gaps): Mean accuracy stays high (~0.95–0.99) across change rounds (1, 3, 5, 10). There is a slight dip around the 3-round point (short_term/long_term labels near ~0.96–0.97), a recovery at 5 rounds (~0.99), then a small decline by 10 rounds (~0.95–0.96). Overall, degradation is mild and non-monotonic, suggesting either (a) stochastic trial variability dominates, (b) the composition of change types differs by horizon, or (c) the system’s matching logic is stable but occasionally fails when multiple edits touch the same records across rounds. For ablation narrative: temporal gap alone is not a major driver of failure on synthetic data; the rare errors at extended horizons should be traced to specific change sequences (e.g., modify-then-delete, schema-change-then-modify) to show where the pipeline breaks.

Change type detection performance across latencies (bar chart): Additions and deletions F1 are essentially perfect (~0.99–1.0) for all latency configurations. Modifications F1 is consistently lower and shows the clearest sensitivity to longer latencies: ~0.98–0.99 for immediate/short/medium, dropping to ~0.96 at long_term, and recovering slightly (~0.97) at extended_term. This indicates modifications are the hardest change type, likely due to record alignment/identity resolution when multiple versions exist or when modifications interact with other operations. Key takeaway: if you need a single place to invest engineering effort, it is modification detection (e.g., robust keying, canonicalization, tolerance for formatting changes), since additions/deletions are already saturated.

Metrics heatmap across configurations and trials (Overall Accuracy, Modifications F1, Average F1): Across trials T1–T5, immediate configuration shows perfect or near-perfect metrics early (Overall Acc 1.0 at T1–T3) but drops at later trials (e.g., Overall Acc ~0.98 at T4 and ~0.93 at T5; Modifications F1 similarly ~0.97 at T4 and ~0.95 at T5). short_term has one notably low Overall Accuracy at T2 (~0.94) while other trials are ~0.96–1.0; Modifications F1 at T2 ~0.96. medium_term is consistently strong (Overall Acc ~0.96–0.99; Avg F1 ~0.98–1.0). long_term is slightly lower and steadier (Overall Acc ~0.94–0.97; Modifications F1 dips to ~0.94 in some trials). extended_term shows a conspicuous low Overall Accuracy at T5 (~0.90) while other trials are ~0.96–0.98; Modifications F1 at T5 ~0.94. Interpretation: variability is trial-driven with occasional outliers, not a uniform latency effect. This suggests certain random seeds/change realizations are adversarial (e.g., many modifications on same keys, chained edits). For a top-conference story, you can mine these outlier trials to produce qualitative failure case studies and motivate impact analysis thresholds/alerts.

Duplicate metrics heatmap (same as previous): Appears to replicate the same three heatmaps with identical values/patterns. If intentional, it could be used to show reproducibility across runs; but as presented it likely indicates a plotting duplication. Recommendation: remove redundancy or explicitly label as “replicate run” with statistical aggregation (mean±std) to strengthen claims.

Duplicate change-type bar chart (same as earlier): The plot repeats the earlier bar chart with the same pattern—additions/deletions near-perfect, modifications slightly lower at longer latencies. As with the duplicated heatmap, either consolidate or label as an independent replicate and report variance.

Performance–accuracy trade-off (hash functions scatter, time vs F1): All points sit at F1=1.0 across a wide runtime range (~0.1s to ~12s), meaning the scatter primarily reflects dataset size rather than algorithmic trade-offs. Hash functions overlap heavily; no clear Pareto frontier among md5/sha1/sha256/simple_concat because accuracy is identical and time differences are tiny. This plot supports the conclusion that, under these synthetic conditions, you should pick the simplest/fastest hash (or one with desired collision properties/security) without sacrificing detection performance. For a more informative trade-off, include (i) larger sizes (>=10^5), (ii) more complex record structures, (iii) normalization/canonicalization costs, and (iv) collision/near-duplicate stress tests so that accuracy can vary.

Component contribution analysis (F1 drop / time ratio) when removing change-type detectors: Removing additions or deletions yields a large F1 drop (ΔF1≈0.333 each) with high contribution scores (~0.333–0.334), indicating these components are essential to overall F1 under the averaging scheme used. Removing modifications shows ΔF1≈0.333 but contribution score ~0.000, implying the time ratio term dominates (e.g., removing modifications does not reduce time, or the ratio is computed in a way that cancels). Removing schema_changes shows ΔF1≈0.000 and contribution ~0.000, suggesting schema change detection either was not exercised in this benchmark or contributes negligibly to the chosen metric. Important caveat: the metric “F1 drop / time ratio” can be misleading if time savings are ~0 or if time ratio is ill-defined; also, if overall F1 is macro-averaged over three types (add/del/mod), dropping one type will mechanically reduce macro-F1 by ~1/3 even if the remaining detectors are perfect. For meaningful component attribution, report per-type F1 and micro-averaged F1, plus actual wall-clock deltas when disabling each component.

Detection Accuracy vs Total Changes (Scalability Analysis): This plot appears visually identical to the earlier “Detection Accuracy vs Total Changes” scatter (same clusters and ranges). If it is meant to be a separate scalability run, it should include explicit dataset size/density annotations or time/throughput on a secondary axis. import os
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from datetime import datetime
import hashlib
import json

# Setup
working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")
os.makedirs(working_dir, exist_ok=True)

# Initialize experiment data tracking
experiment_data = {
    "modification_depth": {
        "synthetic_dataset": {
            "metrics": {},
            "ground_truth": {},
            "predictions": {},
            "timestamps": {},
        }
    }
}

# Step 1: Create synthetic dataset versions with known changes
np.random.seed(42)


def create_base_dataset(n_samples=1000):
    """Create a base dataset version"""
    data = {
        "id": np.arange(n_samples),
        "feature_1": np.random.randn(n_samples),
        "feature_2": np.random.randint(0, 100, n_samples),
        "feature_3": np.random.choice(["A", "B", "C"], n_samples),
        "feature_4": np.random.randn(n_samples),
        "feature_5": np.random.randint(0, 50, n_samples),
        "feature_6": np.random.choice(["X", "Y", "Z"], n_samples),
        "target": np.random.randint(0, 2, n_samples),
    }
    return pd.DataFrame(data)


def introduce_changes_with_depth(df_base, change_config, modification_depth):
    """Introduce known changes with specified modification depth per row"""
    df_v2 = df_base.copy()
    ground_truth_changes = {
        "additions": [],
        "deletions": [],
        "modifications": [],
        "schema_changes": [],
    }

    # Available columns excluding id and target
    available_cols = [col for col in df_base.columns if col not in ["id", "target"]]

    # 1. Add new rows
    n_additions = change_config["n_additions"]
    if n_additions > 0:
        new_rows = {
            "id": np.arange(len(df_base), len(df_base) + n_additions),
            "feature_1": np.random.randn(n_additions),
            "feature_2": np.random.randint(0, 100, n_additions),
            "feature_3": np.random.choice(["A", "B", "C"], n_additions),
            "feature_4": np.random.randn(n_additions),
            "feature_5": np.random.randint(0, 50, n_additions),
            "feature_6": np.random.choice(["X", "Y", "Z"], n_additions),
            "target": np.random.randint(0, 2, n_additions),
        }
        df_new = pd.DataFrame(new_rows)
        df_v2 = pd.concat([df_v2, df_new], ignore_index=True)
        ground_truth_changes["additions"] = list(new_rows["id"])

    # 2. Delete rows
    n_deletions = change_config["n_deletions"]
    if n_deletions > 0:
        delete_indices = np.random.choice(
            df_base.index[: len(df_base)], n_deletions, replace=False
        )
        deleted_ids = df_base.loc[delete_indices, "id"].tolist()
        df_v2 = df_v2[~df_v2["id"].isin(deleted_ids)]
        ground_truth_changes["deletions"] = deleted_ids

    # 3. Modify existing rows with specified depth
    n_modifications = change_config["n_modifications"]
    if n_modifications > 0:
        valid_ids = set(df_base["id"]) - set(ground_truth_changes["deletions"])
        modify_ids = np.random.choice(
            list(valid_ids), min(n_modifications, len(valid_ids)), replace=False
        )

        # Determine actual number of columns to modify based on depth
        if modification_depth == "all":
            num_cols_to_modify = len(available_cols)
        else:
            num_cols_to_modify = min(int(modification_depth), len(available_cols))

        for mod_id in modify_ids:
            idx = df_v2[df_v2["id"] == mod_id].index[0]
            # Randomly select columns to modify
            cols_to_modify = np.random.choice(
                available_cols, num_cols_to_modify, replace=False
            )

            for col in cols_to_modify:
                if df_v2[col].dtype in [np.float64]:
                    df_v2.loc[idx, col] = np.random.randn()
                elif df_v2[col].dtype in [np.int64]:
                    df_v2.loc[idx, col] = np.random.randint(0, 100)
                elif df_v2[col].dtype == object:
                    unique_vals = df_v2[col].dropna().unique()
                    if len(unique_vals) > 0:
                        df_v2.loc[idx, col] = np.random.choice(unique_vals)

        ground_truth_changes["modifications"] = modify_ids.tolist()

    # 4. Schema changes
    if change_config["add_column"]:
        df_v2["feature_new"] = np.random.randn(len(df_v2))
        ground_truth_changes["schema_changes"].append("added_column_feature_new")

    return df_v2, ground_truth_changes


# Step 2: Implement change detection system
class DatasetChangeDetector:
    def __init__(self):
        self.changes_detected = {
            "additions": [],
            "deletions": [],
            "modifications": [],
            "schema_changes": [],
        }

    def compute_row_hash(self, row):
        """Compute hash for a row to detect modifications"""
        row_str = "".join([str(v) for v in row.values])
        return hashlib.md5(row_str.encode()).hexdigest()

    def detect_changes(self, df_v1, df_v2, id_column="id"):
        """Detect all types of changes between two dataset versions"""
        self.changes_detected = {
            "additions": [],
            "deletions": [],
            "modifications": [],
            "schema_changes": [],
        }

        # Detect schema changes
        cols_v1 = set(df_v1.columns)
        cols_v2 = set(df_v2.columns)
        added_cols = cols_v2 - cols_v1
        removed_cols = cols_v1 - cols_v2

        for col in added_cols:
            self.changes_detected["schema_changes"].append(f"added_column_{col}")
        for col in removed_cols:
            self.changes_detected["schema_changes"].append(f"removed_column_{col}")

        # Detect row-level changes
        ids_v1 = set(df_v1[id_column])
        ids_v2 = set(df_v2[id_column])

        # Additions
        self.changes_detected["additions"] = list(ids_v2 - ids_v1)

        # Deletions
        self.changes_detected["deletions"] = list(ids_v1 - ids_v2)

        # Modifications
        common_ids = ids_v1 & ids_v2
        common_cols = cols_v1 & cols_v2

        for common_id in common_ids:
            row_v1 = df_v1[df_v1[id_column] == common_id][list(common_cols)].iloc[0]
            row_v2 = df_v2[df_v2[id_column] == common_id][list(common_cols)].iloc[0]

            hash_v1 = self.compute_row_hash(row_v1)
            hash_v2 = self.compute_row_hash(row_v2)

            if hash_v1 != hash_v2:
                self.changes_detected["modifications"].append(common_id)

        return self.changes_detected


# Step 3: Evaluate change detection accuracy
def evaluate_change_detection(ground_truth, predictions):
    """Evaluate change detection performance"""
    metrics = {}

    for change_type in ["additions", "deletions", "modifications", "schema_changes"]:
        gt = set(ground_truth.get(change_type, []))
        pred = set(predictions.get(change_type, []))

        if len(gt) == 0 and len(pred) == 0:
            metrics[f"{change_type}_accuracy"] = 1.0
            metrics[f"{change_type}_precision"] = 1.0
            metrics[f"{change_type}_recall"] = 1.0
            metrics[f"{change_type}_f1"] = 1.0
        elif len(gt) == 0:
            metrics[f"{change_type}_accuracy"] = 0.0
            metrics[f"{change_type}_precision"] = 0.0
            metrics[f"{change_type}_recall"] = 1.0
            metrics[f"{change_type}_f1"] = 0.0
        else:
            tp = len(gt & pred)
            fp = len(pred - gt)
            fn = len(gt - pred)

            precision = tp / (tp + fp) if (tp + fp) > 0 else 0.0
            recall = tp / (tp + fn) if (tp + fn) > 0 else 0.0
            f1 = (
                2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall)
                if (precision + recall) > 0
                else 0.0
            )
            accuracy = tp / len(gt) if len(gt) > 0 else 0.0

            metrics[f"{change_type}_accuracy"] = accuracy
            metrics[f"{change_type}_precision"] = precision
            metrics[f"{change_type}_recall"] = recall
            metrics[f"{change_type}_f1"] = f1

    # Overall change detection accuracy
    all_gt = set()
    all_pred = set()
    for change_type in ["additions", "deletions", "modifications"]:
        all_gt.update([f"{change_type}_{x}" for x in ground_truth.get(change_type, [])])
        all_pred.update(
            [f"{change_type}_{x}" for x in predictions.get(change_type, [])]
        )

    if len(all_gt) > 0:
        overall_accuracy = len(all_gt & all_pred) / len(all_gt)
    else:
        overall_accuracy = 1.0

    metrics["overall_change_detection_accuracy"] = overall_accuracy

    return metrics

I will create visualizations for the modification depth ablation study, showing how the number of modified columns per row affects change detection performance across different metrics and configurations.

The experiment tests 6 different modification depths (1, 2, 3, 4, 6, and 'all' columns) across 3 different change configurations. I'll plot the overall accuracy, modification-specific metrics (precision, recall, F1), performance across configurations, heatmaps of all metrics, and trend analysis to understand how modification depth impacts detection effectiveness.

plot_analyses.analysis:
Hash function component analysis (F1 vs dataset size; execution time vs dataset size at density=1% and 10%): F1 is flat at 1.00 for all hash functions (md5/sha1/sha256/simple_concat) across dataset sizes 10^2, 10^3, 10^4 for both densities shown. This indicates the detection logic is insensitive to the hash choice in these synthetic settings—no accuracy trade-off is observed.

Runtime scales sharply with dataset size (roughly linear-to-superlinear visually, but at least monotonic): ~0.1–0.4s at 100 rows, ~1.2–1.3s at 1k, ~12s at 10k for both densities. Differences between hash functions are small; simple_concat appears marginally fastest at 10k, while sha256 is slightly slower at 10k under density=10%. Density (1% vs 10%) barely affects runtime, suggesting the implementation cost is dominated by full-pass hashing/comparison rather than number of detected changes. Implication: for a minimal viable system, prefer the simplest/fastest hash (or even non-cryptographic) unless collision resistance is a requirement; the plots don’t show any accuracy penalty for simple_concat.

Caveat: Because all F1 values are saturated at 1.0, these experiments may not stress collision scenarios, near-duplicate rows, or schema edge cases; to claim robustness, you’ll need adversarial or high-cardinality tests where hash collisions/normalization matter.

Change type detection / combinations (bar chart: mean overall accuracy by change-type combination; heatmap: F1 by change type and combination): Mean overall accuracy is 1.0 for every change-type combination (single types, pairs, triples, and all four). The dashed reference line at 1.0 is matched by all bars, implying no measurable degradation when multiple change types co-occur.

The F1 heatmap likewise shows 1.00 across all change types (additions, deletions, modifications, schema_changes) and across all combinations, indicating perfect per-type detection under these synthetic conditions.

Implication for component isolation: with these results, disabling any single change-type detector would be expected to reduce accuracy only on datasets where that change exists; however, the current plots do not include the ablated runs (e.g., “modifications disabled”)—they only show performance when all relevant detectors are enabled. To quantify contribution, you’ll need explicit ablation curves: run each dataset with one detector disabled and report per-type and overall metrics; otherwise, the conclusion is simply that the full system handles combinations without interference.

Caveat: Saturation at 1.0 suggests the benchmark may be too easy (clear keys, deterministic diffs). Consider adding ambiguous cases: row reordering without stable IDs, type casting (int↔str), missing-value normalization, and partial schema evolution (rename vs drop+add) to reveal failure modes.

Modification depth ablation (bar: mean detection accuracy vs modification depth; heatmaps: modifications F1/precision/recall/overall accuracy by depth and config; line plot across configurations; grouped bars precision/recall/F1 vs depth): This is the only set showing a meaningful drop: at depth=1 column modified per row, mean detection accuracy is ~0.80 (with error bars), substantially below the overall mean line (~0.961). At depth=2, accuracy rises to ~0.96; at depth≥3 (3,4,6, all) accuracy reaches ~1.0.

The metric breakdown explains why: precision stays at 1.00 across depths/configs, while recall is the limiting factor at depth=1 (≈0.64–0.66 across configs), improving at depth=2 (≈0.91–0.98) and reaching 1.00 at depth≥3. Consequently, modifications F1 at depth=1 is ~0.78–0.80, depth=2 ~0.95–0.99, and depth≥3 is 1.00.

Across configurations (config_1/2/3), depth=1 accuracy varies (~0.78–0.82), but the qualitative pattern is consistent: single-column modifications are the hard case.

The “column modification intensity” summary bar shows mean accuracy ~0.879 for 1_column, ~0.987 for 2_columns, and 1.0 for 3/5/all columns. The scatter plot reinforces a positive relationship: as average columns modified per row increases (≈0.6–0.8 up to 6), accuracy increases and saturates at 1.0.

Interpretation: The system is conservative (no false positives) but misses subtle modifications when only one field changes—suggesting either (a) a thresholding/heuristic that requires multiple-field evidence, (b) a normalization step that collapses small changes, (c) a join/keying issue where modified rows are not aligned reliably unless changes are “large enough,” or (d) evaluation labeling that treats some single-column edits as modifications but the detector classifies them as unchanged.

Actionable follow-ups for a top-conference ablation story:
- Report per-change-type confusion matrices specifically for modifications at depth=1 to localize misses.
- Vary magnitude of single-column edits (tiny numeric perturbations, whitespace, casing) to see if misses correlate with normalization.
- If hashing is row-level over concatenated columns, ensure stable serialization; single-column edits should flip the hash—if not, there may be truncation/canonicalization or a bug.

Minimal viable system insight from these plots: you can likely keep a single fast hash and still get perfect results for additions/deletions/schema changes and for modifications affecting ≥2–3 columns; but to guarantee ≥95% overall when modifications are sparse/subtle (depth=1), you need an enhanced modification detector (e.g., field-level hashing/diffing, lower thresholds, or better row alignment).

Key findings: all hash functions achieve saturated F1=1.0; runtime scales strongly with dataset size and is nearly density-invariant, with only minor hash-dependent differences. Change-type combinations show perfect accuracy/F1 across all combinations (no interference). The only non-trivial failure mode is modifications when only 1 column changes: precision remains 1.0 but recall drops to ~0.64–0.66, yielding ~0.78–0.80 F1 and ~0.80 accuracy; performance recovers to ≥0.95 by 2 columns and reaches 1.0 for ≥3 columns.

import os
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from datetime import datetime
import hashlib
import json

# Setup
working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")
os.makedirs(working_dir, exist_ok=True)

# Initialize experiment data tracking
experiment_data = {
    "multi_version_analysis": {
        "version_chain_1": {
            "metrics": {},
            "ground_truth": {},
            "predictions": {},
            "timestamps": {},
        },
        "version_chain_2": {
            "metrics": {},
            "ground_truth": {},
            "predictions": {},
            "timestamps": {},
        },
        "version_chain_3": {
            "metrics": {},
            "ground_truth": {},
            "predictions": {},
            "timestamps": {},
        },
    }
}

np.random.seed(42)


def create_base_dataset(n_samples=1000):
    """Create a base dataset version"""
    data = {
        "id": np.arange(n_samples),
        "feature_1": np.random.randn(n_samples),
        "feature_2": np.random.randint(0, 100, n_samples),
        "feature_3": np.random.choice(["A", "B", "C"], n_samples),
        "feature_4": np.random.randn(n_samples),
        "feature_5": np.random.randint(0, 50, n_samples),
        "target": np.random.randint(0, 2, n_samples),
    }
    return pd.DataFrame(data)


def introduce_changes_versioned(df_base, change_config, exclude_id_col="id"):
    """Introduce changes to create a new version with detailed tracking"""
    df_new = df_base.copy()
    ground_truth_changes = {
        "additions": [],
        "deletions": [],
        "modifications": [],
        "modification_details": {},
    }

    # Add new rows
    n_additions = change_config.get("n_additions", 0)
    if n_additions > 0:
        max_id = df_base["id"].max()
        new_rows = {
            "id": np.arange(max_id + 1, max_id + 1 + n_additions),
            "feature_1": np.random.randn(n_additions),
            "feature_2": np.random.randint(0, 100, n_additions),
            "feature_3": np.random.choice(["A", "B", "C"], n_additions),
            "feature_4": np.random.randn(n_additions),
            "feature_5": np.random.randint(0, 50, n_additions),
            "target": np.random.randint(0, 2, n_additions),
        }
        df_new_rows = pd.DataFrame(new_rows)
        df_new = pd.concat([df_new, df_new_rows], ignore_index=True)
        ground_truth_changes["additions"] = list(new_rows["id"])

    # Delete rows
    n_deletions = change_config.get("n_deletions", 0)
    if n_deletions > 0:
        available_ids = df_base["id"].values
        delete_ids = np.random.choice(
            available_ids, min(n_deletions, len(available_ids)), replace=False
        )
        df_new = df_new[~df_new["id"].isin(delete_ids)]
        ground_truth_changes["deletions"] = delete_ids.tolist()

    # Modify existing rows
    n_modifications = change_config.get("n_modifications", 0)
    if n_modifications > 0:
        valid_ids = set(df_base["id"]) - set(ground_truth_changes["deletions"])
        modify_ids = np.random.choice(
            list(valid_ids), min(n_modifications, len(valid_ids)), replace=False
        )

        for mod_id in modify_ids:
            idx = df_new[df_new["id"] == mod_id].index[0]
            modified_cols = []

            # Modify random columns
            modifiable_cols = [col for col in df_new.columns if col != "id"]
            n_cols_to_modify = np.random.randint(1, max(2, len(modifiable_cols) // 2))
            cols_to_modify = np.random.choice(
                modifiable_cols, n_cols_to_modify, replace=False
            )

            for col in cols_to_modify:
                if df_new[col].dtype in [np.float64]:
                    df_new.loc[idx, col] = np.random.randn()
                elif df_new[col].dtype in [np.int64]:
                    df_new.loc[idx, col] = np.random.randint(0, 100)
                elif df_new[col].dtype == object:
                    unique_vals = df_new[col].dropna().unique()
                    if len(unique_vals) > 0:
                        df_new.loc[idx, col] = np.random.choice(unique_vals)
                modified_cols.append(col)

            ground_truth_changes["modifications"].append(mod_id)
            ground_truth_changes["modification_details"][mod_id] = modified_cols

    return df_new, ground_truth_changes


class DatasetChangeDetector:
    def __init__(self):
        self.changes_detected = {
            "additions": [],
            "deletions": [],
            "modifications": [],
        }

    def compute_row_hash(self, row):
        """Compute hash for a row to detect modifications"""
        row_str = "".join([str(v) for v in row.values])
        return hashlib.md5(row_str.encode()).hexdigest()

    def detect_changes(self, df_v1, df_v2, id_column="id"):
        """Detect all types of changes between two dataset versions"""
        self.changes_detected = {
            "additions": [],
            "deletions": [],
            "modifications": [],
        }

        # Detect row-level changes
        ids_v1 = set(df_v1[id_column])
        ids_v2 = set(df_v2[id_column])

        # Additions
        self.changes_detected["additions"] = list(ids_v2 - ids_v1)

        # Deletions
        self.changes_detected["deletions"] = list(ids_v1 - ids_v2)

        # Modifications
        common_ids = ids_v1 & ids_v2
        common_cols = set(df_v1.columns) & set(df_v2.columns)

        for common_id in common_ids:
            row_v1 = df_v1[df_v1[id_column] == common_id][list(common_cols)].iloc[0]
            row_v2 = df_v2[df_v2[id_column] == common_id][list(common_cols)].iloc[0]

            hash_v1 = self.compute_row_hash(row_v1)
            hash_v2 = self.compute_row_hash(row_v2)

            if hash_v1 != hash_v2:
                self.changes_detected["modifications"].append(common_id)

        return self.changes_detected


def evaluate_change_detection(ground_truth, predictions):
    """Evaluate change detection performance"""
    metrics = {}

    for change_type in ["additions", "deletions", "modifications"]:
        gt = set(ground_truth.get(change_type, []))
        pred = set(predictions.get(change_type, []))

        if len(gt) == 0 and len(pred) == 0:
            metrics[f"{change_type}_precision"] = 1.0
            metrics[f"{change_type}_recall"] = 1.0
            metrics[f"{change_type}_f1"] = 1.0
        elif len(gt) == 0:
            metrics[f"{change_type}_precision"] = 0.0 if len(pred) > 0 else 1.0
            metrics[f"{change_type}_recall"] = 1.0
            metrics[f"{change_type}_f1"] = 0.0
        else:
            tp = len(gt & pred)
            fp = len(pred - gt)
            fn = len(gt - pred)

            precision = tp / (tp + fp) if (tp + fp) > 0 else 0.0
            recall = tp / (tp + fn) if (tp + fn) > 0 else 0.0
            f1 = (
                2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall)
                if (precision + recall) > 0
                else 0.0
            )

            metrics[f"{change_type}_precision"] = precision
            metrics[f"{change_type}_recall"] = recall
            metrics[f"{change_type}_f1"] = f1
            metrics[f"{change_type}_tp"] = tp
            metrics[f"{change_type}_fp"] = fp
            metrics[f"{change_type}_fn"] = fn

    # Overall metrics
    all_gt = set()
    all_pred = set()
    for change_type in ["additions", "deletions", "modifications"]:
        all_gt.update([f"{change_type}_{x}" for x in ground_truth.get(change_type, [])])
        all_pred.update(
            [f"{change_type}_{x}" for x in predictions.get(change_type, [])]
        )

    if len(all_gt) > 0:
        overall_precision = (
            len(all_gt & all_pred) / len(all_pred) if len(all_pred) > 0 else 0.0
        )
        overall_recall = len(all_gt & all_pred) / len(all_gt)
        overall_f1 = (
            2
            * overall_precision
            * overall_recall
            / (overall_precision + overall_recall)
            if (overall_precision + overall_recall) > 0
            else 0.0
        )
    else:
        overall_precision = 1.0 if len(all_pred) == 0 else 0.0
        overall_recall = 1.0
        overall_f1 = 1.0 if len(all_pred) == 0 else 0.0

    metrics["overall_precision"] = overall_precision
    metrics["overall_recall"] = overall_recall
    metrics["overall_f1"] = overall_f1

    return metrics


def compute_transitive_ground_truth(gt_v1_v2, gt_v2_v3):
    """Compute expected ground truth for V1→V3 based on V1→V2→V3 transitions"""
    transitive_gt = {
        "additions": [],
        "deletions": [],
        "modifications": [],
    }

    # Additions: rows added in V2 that survive to V3, plus new rows in V3
    additions_v2 = set(gt_v1_v2.get("additions", []))
    deletions_v3 = set(gt_v2_v3.get("deletions", []))
    additions_v3 = set(gt_v2_v3.get("additions", []))

    # Rows added in V2 and not deleted in V3
    transitive_gt["additions"] = list((additions_v2 - deletions_v3) | additions_v3)

    # Deletions: rows deleted in V2, plus rows that survived V2 but deleted in V3
    deletions_v2 = set(gt_v1_v2.get("deletions", []))
    deletions_from_v1 = set(gt_v2_v3.get("deletions", []))
    transitive_gt["deletions"] = list(deletions_v2 | deletions_from_v1)

    # Modifications: rows modified in either transition (from V1's perspective)
    mods_v2 = set(gt_v1_v2.get("modifications", []))
    mods_v3 = set(gt_v2_v3.get("modifications", []))
    # Any row modified in either step and not deleted
    transitive_gt["modifications"] = list(
        (mods_v2 | mods_v3) - deletions_v3 - deletions_from_v1
    )

    return transitive_gt

plot_plan: This experiment evaluates a hash-based dataset change detection system across multiple version chains (V1→V2→V3), testing how well the detector identifies additions, deletions, and modifications, and whether transitive change detection (V1→V3 direct) degrades compared to pairwise detection. The code will load the experiment data containing metrics, ground truth, and predictions for three version chains with different change patterns, then visualize F1 scores across change types, overall performance degradation, detailed metric heatmaps, and consistency analysis between ground truth and predictions. Key metrics include precision, recall, F1 scores for each change type, and the degradation between average pairwise F1 and direct transitive F1 scores.
